source.title: Build An AI Second Brain Knowledge Base (Step-By-Step)

collection.scope: public comments and replies accessible without authentication

comments.text:
I took this transcript and the Karpathy Tweet and the other link you gave of his into a new Claude project. I then asked Claude to build a course for my ADHD brain to learn and build out a Second Brain tailored to my needs. 
It created a 10 module course for me. I’m a new content creator here on YouTube and on SubStack. I’m live with very late diagnosed ADHD and am 70. Thanks so much for this!

comments.text: The biggest takeaway for me from this one wasn't the AI, it was the markdown-first design. The idea of building a durable knowledge layer that survives whatever AI tool comes next feels much more important than any specific model. Looking forward to working through the rest of the playlist and seeing where you take it.

comments.text: Neat setup. Worth noting for the CRM, there are open-source CRMs (Twenty is one) that self-host and expose an MCP server, so the agent could write contacts straight into a real CRM instead of markdown. Obsidian still makes sense for the wiki/journal. Just an alternative for folks who want structured contact data.

comments.text:
The most interesting challenge isn't building the wiki. It's deciding what becomes durable memory, what stays provisional, and what should eventually be archived or superseded.

Once the vault gets large, memory governance becomes more important than ingestion.

comments.text:
My system of AI, Ra, saw your vid and had a word of warning...

Ra:

"Big caution

His system lets AI update the wiki as it answers questions. That is powerful, but dangerous.

Bad memory can spread like mold.

So for us, the rule should be:

Raw is sacred. Processed is provisional. Canon requires confirmation.

Meaning:

Raw transcripts stay untouched.
AI summaries can be useful but are not canon.
Crossroads/Morterth/Studio-Ra entries only become canon when you explicitly say to file/update them.
Every processed note should preserve source links and date.
Every inferred idea should be marked as inference.

That keeps the Vault from becoming haunted soup."

comments.text: Osmosis just took over in a Twilight Zone sort of way! In the last few weeks I’ve been looking for a way to move from the DB contestants that have been slowing me down in Notion to a system tailored more to the way I think and work – and have felt Obsidian was the way to go. So many other YTers are pushing PARA and folders within folders – while I’m discovering a more Link/Tag scenario within Obsidian would better serve my needs. I’ve struggled with the initial setup though. Yesterday I had a ‘chat’ wth ChatGPT about this very topic of using Codex (I’m a newb at this part) and trying to set up a flat file File Management System. From my initial conversation with GPT came a very basic, but expandable, framework – and one that I was about to dive into today. Until. From a couple of quick readings in Medium on Obsidian, to a search on YT – all of a sudden up popped your most recent video here. Seriously – this edit 33 minute video you just posted has saved me hours/days/weeks?? of diving down all too many rabbit holes. This is exactly what I was looking for. Osmosis?? Damn right! Thanks Matt!
comments.author: @TheDeliveryDoctor

comments.text: Very interesting video and use case. BUT you should NOT be uploading this to GitHub. If you're using this as your second brain, you're letting Microsoft and the US government literally read your mind. This should be kept 100% local. Even more so if you're including dossiers on other people. It's one thing to accept giving up your own privacy, but you have a responsibility to handle other people's information with care. Please keep that in mind.

comments.text:
{
  "name": "Personal_AI_Kernel",
  "version": "0.1",
  "purpose": "Help AI respond to this user with continuity, clarity, and low cognitive load.",
  "identity_model": {
    "thinking_style": "systems-level, pattern-seeking, prefers structure and compression",
    "constraints": "gets overwhelmed by long technical explanations; needs step-by-step guidance",
    "preferences": "concise, direct, practical, structured, minimal filler"
  },
  "system_priority": [
    "reduce cognitive load",
    "maximize clarity",
    "avoid multi-direction answers",
    "turn abstract ideas into practical steps"
  ],
  "interaction_rules": [
    "answer in short structured sections",
    "give one next step at a time",
    "avoid unnecessary theory",
    "flag uncertainty",
    "use examples when useful",
    "do not flatter; show respect by taking the user seriously"
  ],
  "execution_layer": {
    "response_protocol": [
      "identify task type",
      "reduce scope to one clear output",
      "structure response",
      "remove filler",
      "end with one practical next step"
    ],
    "failure_modes": [
      "too long -> compress",
      "too abstract -> make practical",
      "too technical -> explain simply",
      "too many options -> choose best path"
    ]
  }
}

comments.text: I did something similar starting with Obsidian and Co-work so its a little more manual. My next iteration will be good to build in the content consumption and automate it more so your video was really useful. Each day a research question is generated based on topics being added, vault gaps and a penalty for a past question to try and get new topic questions each day. The question gets passed to ChatGPT for research and then the response markdown gets ingested back in with a comments file from me by Claude. I'm currently working on putting the sources into Zotero and then having a citation table in Obsidian so it is quite possible I will never write an essay and just spend my time refining the system, in the style of Rimmer from Red Dwarf studying for his engineering/astronavigation exam. 'I am a fish!'
comments.author: @thoughtgale

comments.text: just a small things - the bigger is the file/memory the more connections it will have and therefore the more ai agents will burn tokens to read it. So some small question can burn through few thousand tokens just to answer things. so be aware of that.
comments.author: @Jokershadow696

comments.text: Is it possible to tackle this issue, within obsidian?
comments.author: @miikalewandowski7765

comments.text: ​ @miikalewandowski7765  I haven't looked too deep into that but is the price you pay for having all info connected to each other and ability to get answer fast without agent reading every single file fully. I would say just think how big the wiki folder might be and divide it into smaller wiki folders dedicated to specific category which doesn't need to be connected to each other. I personally created my own wiki with instructions how and why to use each folder with files inside of them etc. So i don't need info from marketing folder to be connected to news research folder or video editing folder etc. I connect only the categories that require that connection.
comments.author: @Jokershadow696
